Supplementary Material for Submission #4594

Additional figures and tables referenced in the author response

This page accompanies the rebuttal and will be updated during the discussion period.

Figure 1 — Window sensitivity on Broderick2019

Figure 1. Window sensitivity ablation on Broderick2019 (phoneme detection). F1 Event (IoU ≥ 0.5) is reported as a function of window duration W, averaged over 5 cross-validation folds and 18 subjects. Two experimental conditions are compared: (i) scaling the number of queries Nq proportionally to W so that the event-to-query ratio remains constant at ≈ 0.48 (blue, solid), and (ii) holding Nq = 150 fixed while varying W, which changes the ratio from 0.12 to 1.89 (red, dashed).

Table 1. Window configuration and Perceiver resolution across all datasets. The Perceiver module resamples every window into 256 latent tokens regardless of duration. Tokens/Event is computed as 256 / n̄events and must remain ≥ 3 for accurate boundary regression.

Figure 2 — Sensitivity analysis across datasets

Figure 2. Sensitivity analysis of DANCE across 10 datasets. (A) F1event as a function of the IoU matching threshold τ. Curves are colored by task category (blue: Motor Imagery, green: Speech, purple: Typing, orange: Artifacts, red: P300, teal: Seizure). Shaded bands denote ±1 SEM across subjects. (B) Joint view of detection quality (F1detect, y-axis) and temporal precision (center prediction error in seconds, x-axis, log scale) for DANCE (colored circles) versus the best non-ablation baseline (gray diamonds). Arrows point from baseline to DANCE. Upper-left is better on both axes.

DANCEBest Baseline
DatasetBest BaselineF1detF1evtΔctr (s)F1detF1evtΔctr (s)
BNCI2014CBraMod0.9090.3980.0780.6270.2120.277
BNCI2015CBraMod0.8430.5730.1700.2890.1970.540
SparrKULeeCBraMod0.4110.4110.0740.1940.1940.806
BroderickCBraMod0.4460.4460.0140.1450.1450.260

BI2014bCBraMod0.5290.3920.1210.1040.0970.347
TUSZCBraMod0.3530.2104.5220.0210.0123.878
Table 2. Decoupled detection and localization metrics for DANCE and the best non-ablation baseline per dataset (IoU ≥ 0.5). F1detect measures class-agnostic event detection (“was an event found?”). F1event additionally requires correct classification. Δcenter is the mean absolute temporal error between predicted and ground-truth event centers, in seconds, computed over true positives. Table 2 confirms that DANCE outperforms the best baselines on both F1 metrics across all 10 datasets, while simultaneously achieving 2–18× lower center prediction errors on 9 out of 10 tasks.

Figure 3 — EEGSym vs. DANCE comparison

Figure 3. Comparison of DANCE and EEGSym on the BNCI2014 Motor Imagery dataset (9 subjects, 5-fold cross-validation). We evaluate two variants of EEGSym: classic, which produces a single class prediction per window and relies on timeline reconstruction to assign per-timestep labels, and dense, a minimal adaptation that outputs per-timestep predictions. (A) F1 Sample (macro-averaged multiclass F1 over timesteps): DANCE achieves 50.8%, compared to 13.8% and 15.2% for EEGSym classic and dense respectively (3.3× improvement). (B) F1 Event (IoU-F1 at 0.5 with class matching): DANCE achieves 39.8%, while EEGSym classic fails entirely (0.0%) and the dense variant reaches only 2.5% (16× improvement). Error bars denote SEM across subjects. These results demonstrate that models designed for single-window classification cannot be trivially adapted to continuous event detection, validating the need for DANCE’s set-prediction formulation.

Figure 4 — CBraMod BI2014b training curves

Figure 4. Training curves for CBraMod on the BI2014b dataset. Top: BNCI2014 (4-class: Tongue, Feet, Left Hand, Right Hand) showing a 70 s excerpt from a held-out test session (subject 3). Bottom: BNCI2015 (2-class: Feet, Right Hand) showing a 300 s excerpt (subject 9), illustrating performance at a longer time scale. Each row displays the ground-truth (GT) events and predictions from DANCE, CBraMod, and U-Net. Solid borders denote correct detections (prediction overlaps a same-class GT event by more than 50%), while dashed borders indicate false positives. Event-level F1 (F1e) and sample-level F1 (F1s) are reported on the right margin, computed over the full timeline. DANCE consistently recovers both the temporal location and class of events, achieving F1e/F1s of 0.61/0.70 on BNCI2014 and 0.44/0.56 on BNCI2015, whereas CBraMod and U-Net produce substantially noisier predictions with F1e below 0.17 on both datasets.

Figure 5 — Loss component weight sensitivity

Figure 5. Sensitivity analysis of loss component weights. Scores are averaged across three datasets (BI2014b, BNCI2014, Broderick2019) using per-subject metrics aggregated over 5-fold cross-validation. Error bars denote standard error of the mean across datasets.

Figure 6 — Decoded event timelines on motor imagery datasets

Figure 6. Qualitative comparison of decoded event timelines on two motor imagery BCI datasets. Top: BNCI2014 (4-class: Tongue, Feet, Left Hand, Right Hand) showing a 70 s excerpt from a held-out test session. Bottom: BNCI2015 (2-class: Feet, Right Hand) showing a 300 s excerpt, illustrating performance at a longer time scale. Each row displays the ground-truth (GT) events and predictions from DANCE, CBraMod, and U-Net. Solid borders denote correct detections (prediction overlaps a same-class GT event by more than 50%), while dashed borders indicate false positives. Event-level F1 (F1e) and sample-level F1 (F1s) are reported on the right margin, computed over the full timeline.
